nativeRange.habitat: Slow-flowing blackwater rivers, streams, and swampy areas with dense marginal and aquatic vegetation, leaf litter, and woody structures; water is heavily tea-stained and extremely soft

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java moss, floating plants, Echinodorus, Microsorum)
dried leaf litter (Indian almond/catappa leaves) to stain water and lower pH
driftwood and twisted roots for cover
dark substrate to bring out coloration
low flow — sponge filter or gentle hang-on recommended

diet.notes: Micropredator in nature, feeding on tiny invertebrates and zooplankton. In captivity accepts high-quality micro-pellets and finely crushed flake, but thrives best with daily offerings of appropriately sized live and frozen foods such as baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), Moina, micro worms, daphnia, and grindal worms. Small mouth size rules out standard flake or large pellets.

goodWith: small peaceful characids of similar size
dwarf cichlids kept with caution (e.g. Apistogramma in larger setups)
ember tetras and other nano tetras
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving community fish (outcompete for food)
any fish large enough to swallow them
cherry shrimp and small dwarf shrimp (fry and shrimplets at risk)

summary: The Barred Pencilfish is a Guyana endemic distinguished by five permanent dark vertical bars — unique among pencilfish, which nearly all bear horizontal stripes. A delicate blackwater nano species requiring soft, acidic conditions and micro-sized foods; best kept as a species group of 8–10 in a well-planted biotope.
careNotes:
Nannostomus espei is the only pencilfish to display a permanent pattern of dark vertical bars (or chevron-like blotches) along the flanks — in contrast to the horizontal stripes characteristic of nearly all other Nannostomus species. Unlike most congeners it shows relatively little difference between its daytime and nighttime coloration, making it distinctive even under aquarium lighting. It is endemic to the Mazaruni River system in Guyana, where it inhabits extremely soft, acidic blackwater with pH values that can fall below 5.0 and negligible mineral content.

In the aquarium, water quality is paramount. The tank should be mature and well-seasoned before introduction; N. espei is sensitive to elevated nitrates and to sudden parameter shifts. Target pH 4.5–6.5 with gH below 5 dH; reverse-osmosis water buffered with catappa leaves or peat filtration is strongly recommended in hard-water areas. Temperature 22–28°C is tolerated, with 24–26°C preferred. Gentle flow from a sponge filter or air-driven box filter is ideal — these fish are poor swimmers relative to larger species and originate from near-still microhabitats.

A group of at least 8–10 individuals is recommended to distribute male-to-male fin-nipping and to encourage natural shoaling behavior. Males may spar with each other but serious damage is rare in adequately sized groups. The species occupies mid-water and upper zones. Due to its very small adult size (~3 cm) and timid nature, it is best kept in a species tank or with only the most sedate and similarly-sized companions. It is not suited to a typical community aquarium where it will be outcompeted for food and potentially harassed.

Feeding requires attention: the mouth is tiny and upturned. Micro-pellets and finely crushed flake may be accepted but live and frozen micro-foods (baby brine shrimp, Moina, daphnia, micro worms) are essential for long-term health and conditioning for breeding. Feed small amounts multiple times daily.
breedingNotes:
Breeding is possible in captivity but considered difficult. A dedicated breeding tank of at least 20–30 L with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–5.5, gH near zero) and dense planting of fine-leaved and broad-leaved species is required. Water temperature around 26–28°C encourages spawning activity. Condition a small group (2 males, 3–4 females) with varied live foods for several weeks before transferring to the breeding setup.

During courtship the male positions himself above the female and nudges her head. The pair deposits 20–40 adhesive eggs on the undersides of broad plant leaves. Parents will readily consume eggs if not removed; either separate the adults post-spawning or use a mesh/grid to prevent access to the bottom. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming after a further 3–4 days. First foods must be extremely fine — infusoria, rotifers, or commercial fry foods such as Hikari First Bites. Baby brine shrimp nauplii can be introduced once fry are large enough, typically around one week. Survival rates are sensitive to water quality; small daily water changes with matched-parameter water improve outcomes.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nannostomus espei
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Mazaruni River system, Guyana), biotope description, water parameters, diet (micropredator — tiny invertebrates and zooplankton), behavior, compatibility guidance, and breeding notes. Confirmed the permanent vertical bar pattern as unique among Nannostomus.

additional.name: FishBase — Nannostomus espei
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Lebiasinidae, Characiformes), adult size (max 4.0 cm TL; typical 2.8 cm), temperature 22–26°C, pH 5.5–7.0, hardness 4–10 dH. Distribution confirmed as Mazaruni River system, Guyana. Reproduction note: males attach 20–40 eggs to underside of leaves.

additional.name: Diszhal.info — Nannostomus espei
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (22–28°C), pH (4–6.5), hardness (1–5 NK° ≈ gH 0–5), adult size (3–4 cm), lifespan (3–5 years), minimum tank volume (50 L). Confirmed breeding difficulty and egg placement behavior. Noted species occupies middle and top water zones.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Colorful Pencils for the Freshwater Aquarium
additional.notes: Noted that N. espei bears five permanent dark comma-shaped blotches, distinguishing it morphologically from congeners. Brief reference confirming the vertical/barred pattern that sets this species apart from the horizontally-striped majority of the genus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pencilfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General pencilfish guidance consulted for diet (small upturned mouths require baby brine shrimp, cyclops, daphnia, crushed flakes) and compatibility notes (avoid cherry/crystal shrimp). Temperature range for pencilfish given as 75–80°F (24–27°C), pH 6.5–7.5; N. espei-specific acidic range from SeriouslyFish and FishBase used for water parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer depositing 20–40 adhesive eggs on underside of broad plant leaves. Requires dedicated tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–5.5, near-zero hardness) and heavy conditioning on live foods. Parents eat eggs; remove adults or use mesh barrier after spawning. Fry demand infusoria or rotifers as first food.
